description: Add multi-patch context window (3 consecutive patches = 24 bytes instead of 1 patch = 8 bytes) with a lightweight single-layer causal self-attention aggregator (ContextAggregator) before the predictor, giving it substantially more sequential context for next-patch prediction while keeping the target side unchanged.

Add cross-patch causal self-attention in the Predictor between SwiGLU blocks 2 and 3, allowing each patch prediction to attend to earlier patches' representations — since patches are consecutive text, knowing what was predicted for patch i provides useful sequential context for predicting patch i+1, improving both MSE alignment and retrieval accuracy. (NO CHANGES)

Widen Predictor SwiGLU hidden dimension from 1x (128→128) to 2x expansion (128→256→128) in both residual blocks, giving the predictor substantially more capacity to learn the current→target mapping without adding new architectural components or changing the forward pass structure.

Add a third residual SwiGLU block (2x expansion) to the Predictor, increasing depth from 2→3 blocks — experiment #3 showed widening helped, now testing whether additional depth gives the predictor richer nonlinear capacity for the current→target mapping.

Upgrade CurrentPatchEncoder from simple ReLU+mean-pool to SwiGLU gating + multi-head attention pooling (4 heads), matching the target encoder's expressive pooling mechanism — the previous mean-pool discarded intra-patch structure that the predictor needs to accurately predict target embeddings.

Switch to symmetric (bidirectional) InfoNCE loss for both EMA-target and online-target contrastive terms (CLIP-style: average pred→target and target→pred cross-entropy), and rebalance loss weights from 0.50/0.35/0.15 to 0.40/0.40/0.20 (mse/infonce/online_infonce) to give more gradient signal toward retrieval accuracy — since retrieval@1 carries 0.25 weight in the probe score and symmetric InfoNCE is known to improve alignment quality by enforcing bidirectional consistency.

Add MoCo-style momentum queue (2048 entries) for InfoNCE contrastive loss — stores recent EMA-target projections as extra negatives, increasing the effective negative set from ~1024 (batch only) to ~3072 (batch + queue), which should improve contrastive discrimination and retrieval accuracy without proportional compute cost. (NO CHANGES)

Add cross-attention layer in the Predictor between the two SwiGLU blocks, allowing the predictor to attend back to all context patch representations (not just the last-position summary) when making its target prediction — this gives the predictor selective access to richer sequential context, which should improve alignment with the target encoder's output since byte patterns often depend on specific earlier context positions.

Widen all three Predictor SwiGLU blocks from 1x to 2x expansion ratio (model_dim→2*model_dim→model_dim, i.e. 128→256→128), giving the predictor substantially more nonlinear capacity to learn the current→target mapping — this same change improved val_bpb in attempt #3, and the architecture has since gained cross-patch attention and better encoders that should benefit even more from wider predictor hidden layers. (NO CHANGES)

Add a second SwiGLU residual block in the TargetPatchEncoder between self-attention and attention pooling — the SA layer enriches byte representations with non-local interactions, but previously these went straight into pooling without nonlinear composition; this extra block lets the model learn complex combinations of SA-enriched features before the irreversible aggregation step, adding capacity to the target encoder path (which hasn't been capacity-expanded yet, unlike the predictor in #3 and current encoder in #5). (NO CHANGES)

Add a contrastive projection head (SwiGLU MLP) to the Predictor that is used only for InfoNCE losses, while MSE stays on the raw prediction — this SimCLR/BYOL pattern lets the contrastive loss shape representations without distorting the direct prediction space that MSE evaluates, improving both alignment metrics.

Add learnable patch-position embeddings to the Predictor so that cross-patch causal self-attention has explicit ordinal position information — currently it only has the causal mask (which encodes order but not distance/position), so patch 0 and patch 3 look identical except for their causal context; position embeddings let the model learn position-dependent prediction strategies (e.g. first-patch predictions are harder and may need different attention patterns than later ones with richer context). (NO CHANGES)

Fix ContextAggregator pre-norm residual connection — previously RMS norm overwrote x before the residual add, so the skip connection carried normalized (information-lossy) values instead of raw patch representations; now the norm only feeds QKV computation while the residual properly bypasses it, improving gradient flow through the 3-patch context aggregation path that feeds the predictor.

Reduce InfoNCE label smoothing from 0.05 to 0.01 for both EMA-target and online-target contrastive losses — with 1024+ negatives (B*num_pairs + 2048 queue), 5% smoothing was distributing excessive probability mass to incorrect targets, diluting the positive retrieval gradient; 1% still regularizes against overconfident predictions while preserving a sharper signal toward correct matches, which should improve retrieval_at_1 (weighted 0.25 in probe_score). (NO CHANGES)

Make InfoNCE losses symmetric (CLIP-style) by averaging pred→target and target→pred cross-entropy directions for both EMA-target and online-target contrastive losses — symmetric contrastive learning improves alignment geometry and directly benefits retrieval accuracy (25% of probe_score), with zero new parameters or architecture changes.

Make ContextAggregator a proper Pre-LN transformer block — fix the residual connection (residual now connects to pre-norm input instead of post-norm, preserving information through the skip path) AND add a SwiGLU FFN after attention, giving the context aggregation both associative mixing (attention) and nonlinear feature composition (FFN) before the predictor consumes the last-position summary. Experiment #13 showed the residual fix alone got within 0.00043 of the best; the added FFN provides the extra representational capacity to cross the threshold.

Switch TargetPatchEncoder's depthwise conv from causal (right-padded, trimmed) to bidirectional (symmetric padding=2) — the target encoder sees complete patches so there's no reason to mask future bytes, and bidirectional local context should produce richer byte-level features for target embeddings. (NO CHANGES)

Increase num_pairs from 4 to 6 in make_patch_pairs — enlarges the InfoNCE contrastive negative set from B×4=1024 to B×6=1536 and gives the Predictor's cross-patch causal self-attention more sequential context (5 preceding patches instead of 3), both of which should improve retrieval accuracy which contributes 0.25 weight to the probe score. (NO CHANGES)

Add direct representation-space InfoNCE loss (weight 0.20) that operates on raw normalized pred/target embeddings (not projection head) — closes the train-eval mismatch where projected-space contrastive losses optimized a different similarity space than the eval retrieval metric (pred_n @ target_n.T), and rebalance loss weights (MSE 0.50→0.45, proj-infonce 0.35→0.25, online 0.15→0.10) to accommodate the new term. (NO CHANGES)

Add intra-patch self-attention (4-head, bidirectional) in TargetPatchEncoder between the depthwise conv and attention pooling — the conv only captures local byte patterns (kernel=5), but with patch_size=8 there are non-local byte dependencies (e.g., byte 0 ↔ byte 7) that require global attention to model; this lets every byte position attend to every other before aggregation, producing richer patch-level target representations.

Add second cross-patch causal self-attention layer in Predictor after SwiGLU block C — the existing single cross-attention (after block B) captures initial sequential dependencies, but with the target encoder now producing richer representations (from attempt #20's intra-patch self-attention), the predictor needs a second round of cross-patch information exchange to fully exploit the improved target signal and refine its predictions before the final feature gate. (NO CHANGES)

Add depthwise bidirectional conv (kernel=5, symmetric padding) with residual connection to CurrentPatchEncoder, matching the pattern proven effective in TargetPatchEncoder — gives the Predictor richer input features capturing local byte n-gram structure, improving its ability to predict target embeddings. (NO CHANGES)

Add intra-patch self-attention (4-head, bidirectional, with learned relative position bias) to CurrentPatchEncoder between conv and attention pooling — mirrors the pattern that improved TargetPatchEncoder in experiment #20, enriching the current-patch representations fed to the Predictor by capturing non-local byte interactions (e.g. byte 0 attending to byte 7) that the k=5 conv cannot reach in patch_size=8, giving the Predictor richer input for next-patch prediction. (NO CHANGES)

Add a separate MLP projection head (model_dim → SiLU → model_dim) for InfoNCE contrastive losses, keeping MSE loss on raw normalized representations — this decouples the contrastive objective from the representation space (SimCLR/BYOL pattern), allowing representations to retain richer information instead of being directly shaped by the contrastive geometry.

Add SwiGLU FFN sub-layer to ContextAggregator after causal self-attention, making it a complete transformer block — the original had attention-only with no FFN, limiting its capacity to nonlinearly combine multi-patch context before the Predictor maps it to target space.

Add cross-attention layer in Predictor between the two SwiGLU blocks — the prediction query (1 token) attends to the full context patch sequence (3 patches) from the ContextAggregator, letting the Predictor recover fine-grained per-patch information that was lost when compressing to a single summary vector. This addresses an information bottleneck where the Predictor had to map from one compressed vector to the target space without access to the richer multi-patch context.

Replace standard cross-entropy in repr_infonce (the loss directly optimizing eval retrieval metric) with focal loss (gamma=2) — down-weights easy negatives and concentrates gradient on hard negatives (high-similarity non-matching patches), and removes label smoothing which hurts retrieval confidence at eval time. No architecture changes; pure loss-shaping targeting the eval metric. (NO CHANGES)

Add MoCo-style momentum queue for raw normalized target embeddings in repr_infonce loss — the repr_infonce directly optimizes the eval retrieval metric (pred_n @ target_n.T) but previously only had B*P in-batch negatives, while the projection-space infonce already benefited from 2048 extra queue negatives; this closes that gap by giving repr_infonce the same 2048 extra negatives, improving contrastive discrimination specifically in the representation space that evaluation measures. | retry: Switch TargetPatchEncoder's depthwise conv from causal (padding=4, right-trimmed) to bidirectional (padding=2, symmetric) since target encoder sees complete patches, and add learnable relative position bias to the intra-patch self-attention so byte positions have explicit distance-aware attention modulation — both changes improve the target encoder's ability to model intra-patch byte structure without adding significant parameters.

Widen InfoNCE projection head hidden dimension from 1x to 2x model_dim (128→256→128) — SimCLR/BYOL literature shows wider projection heads improve contrastive training signal quality by giving the loss a richer intermediate space for discrimination, while the output dim stays the same so queues and eval are unaffected. | retry: Switch TargetPatchEncoder's depthwise conv from causal (padding=4, right-trimmed) to bidirectional (padding=2, symmetric) since the target encoder sees complete patches and has no reason to mask future byte positions, allowing richer local n-gram features from both directions.

Add symmetric (CLIP-style) bidirectional InfoNCE across all three contrastive losses — each loss now averages forward (pred→target) and reverse (target→pred) directions, doubling the contrastive training signal and improving alignment geometry without adding any parameters or meaningful compute cost. | retry: Added SwiGLU FFN sub-layer in TargetPatchEncoder after intra-patch self-attention and before attention pooling, giving the model nonlinear feature composition of SA-enriched byte representations before the irreversible aggregation step — this was attempted in experiment #10 but failed with "no_changes" errors.

Add gated SwiGLU FFN sub-layer (2x expansion) to ContextAggregator with a learnable tanh-gated residual initialized to 0 — the FFN starts as a no-op and gradually learns to contribute, avoiding the early-training destabilization that may have caused #25's near-miss (0.232657 vs best 0.232354).

Replace focal loss (gamma=2) in repr_infonce with standard cross-entropy and rebalance loss weights (MSE 0.45→0.40, repr_infonce 0.20→0.25) — focal loss down-weighted easy positives causing well-learned associations to destabilize, and the weight shift better aligns training with the eval metric which weights retrieval at 0.25. | retry: Switch TargetPatchEncoder's depthwise conv from causal (padding=4, right-trimmed) to bidirectional (padding=2, symmetric) since the target encoder sees complete patches and has no reason to mask future byte positions, allowing richer local n-gram features from both directions within each patch.

Switch TargetPatchEncoder's depthwise conv from causal (padding=4, trim) to bidirectional (padding=2, symmetric) — target patches are fully observed so causal masking wastes half the receptive field; bidirectional conv lets each byte position aggregate context from both neighbors, improving local pattern capture for target embeddings.

Add uniformity loss (Wang & Isola 2020) on prediction embeddings with weight 0.04 — pushes predictions apart on the unit hypersphere via log-mean-exp of pairwise squared distances, complementing the existing per-dimension VICReg variance/covariance losses with a whole-vector geometric anti-collapse signal.

Deepen ContextAggregator from 1-layer to 2-layer causal self-attention (each with its own QKV+out_proj and pre-norm residual) — with 3 context patches, a single attention layer may underfit inter-patch dependencies; a second layer lets the context representation compose higher-order patterns before the predictor transforms it, without touching target encoder or loss weights.

Add SwiGLU FFN sublayers after each attention layer in the 2-layer ContextAggregator (standard transformer block = attention + FFN) — the attention-only layers from exp #35 can mix information across positions but lack per-position nonlinear feature transformation; adding 2x-expansion SwiGLU FFN after each attention sublayer completes the standard transformer block design and should improve context representation quality for the predictor.

Add intra-patch self-attention (4-head, bidirectional) to CurrentPatchEncoder between SwiGLU gating and attention pooling — the current encoder was much simpler than the target encoder (no self-attention, no conv), limiting the quality of context representations fed into the 2-layer ContextAggregator and predictor. Richer per-patch byte interactions in the context encoder should improve prediction quality without touching the target path.

Add cross-attention in Predictor over all context patch positions before the SwiGLU blocks — previously the predictor only saw the last context position's output, discarding per-position information from the 2-layer ContextAggregator; now it queries all 3 context positions via 4-head cross-attention, letting it selectively retrieve relevant context features for target prediction.
Failed to propose

Add a learnable prediction query token appended after context patches in the ContextAggregator — instead of repurposing the last context patch as both context representation and predictor input (with a gated mean-pool patch), a dedicated query token at the final position attends to all context patches via causal masking, cleanly separating "context encoding" from "prediction readout" and giving the model a purpose-built aggregation point for predictor input.

Deepen ContextAggregator from 2-layer to 3-layer causal self-attention — the 1→2 layer jump (exp #35) was the single biggest improvement to date, adding a third attention layer gives the last context position another round of refinement over all preceding patches before being passed to the predictor, with modest parameter growth and no architectural complexity.

Make both InfoNCE losses symmetric (CLIP-style) by averaging pred→target and target→pred cross-entropy directions — the one-directional loss only optimized predictions to match targets, but the reverse direction also encourages target embeddings to be distinguishable from predictions, providing more balanced contrastive gradients without any architecture changes.

Add contrastive projection head to decouple MSE and InfoNCE objective spaces (SimCLR/BYOL-style), and fix ctx_mean_gate parameter missing from optimizer so mean-pooled context blending can actually train — two complementary fixes that reduce objective interference and unlock an unused architectural feature.

Add dropout (p=0.1) after each SwiGLU down-projection in the Predictor's two residual blocks — all recent experiments (#36-#43) added architectural capacity and regressed; this instead adds regularization to the highest-capacity component to reduce train-val gap and improve generalization on held-out patch pairs.

Add SwiGLU FFN blocks after each of the two attention layers in ContextAggregator — standard transformers pair attention with FFN for nonlinear feature composition, but the current aggregator was attention-only, limiting its ability to compose information across context patches before feeding the predictor.

Add second intra-patch self-attention layer (layer 2) in TargetPatchEncoder with pre-norm residual — the 1→2 attention layer deepening pattern was the single biggest improvement for ContextAggregator (exp #35, 0.2340→0.2320), applying the same pattern to TargetPatchEncoder's byte-level self-attention to give richer intra-patch byte interaction modeling before pooling.

Fix ctx_mean_gate parameter missing from optimizer — the zero-initialized gate for blending mean-pooled context into the predictor input was never trained (not in any param group), making it permanently zero/dead; adding it to the optimizer enables the model to learn whether and how much global context pooling helps prediction beyond the last causal position.

Set weight_decay=0 for predictor and log_infonce_temp optimizer groups — predictor maps between representation spaces where WD limits expressiveness (BYOL/MoCo use WD=0 for projectors), and WD on the scalar temperature constantly pushes it toward 0 (temp=1.0) fighting the learned value, both are known anti-patterns in contrastive learning that could be limiting the current best score.

Add cross-attention layer in Predictor that attends over all context patch embeddings before the SwiGLU blocks — instead of the predictor only seeing the last-position summary vector, it now has learned soft attention over all context positions, giving it richer multi-patch context for target prediction without adding encoder capacity (which regressed in exps #41-#46).

Add label smoothing (0.05) to both InfoNCE cross-entropy losses — hard one-hot contrastive labels are noisy when batch negatives include semantically similar patches, and smoothing distributes small probability mass to negatives to prevent overconfident representations and improve generalization without any architecture changes.

Make both InfoNCE losses symmetric by averaging row-wise and column-wise cross-entropy (CLIP-style) — asymmetric contrastive loss only aligns pred→target but not target→pred, leaving retrieval suboptimal; symmetric InfoNCE improves bidirectional alignment with zero parameter overhead.

Detach pred_flat in online_infonce so the predictor only receives gradients from MSE+InfoNCE against the stable EMA target, not conflicting gradients from the still-learning online target encoder — online_infonce now exclusively trains target_online_encoder (its intended purpose) without injecting noisy signal into the predictor

Add learnable per-head relative position bias (4 heads × 15 positions = 60 params) to intra-patch self-attention in TargetPatchEncoder — the current attention relies only on absolute position from local_pos embeddings, but byte-level patterns depend heavily on relative distance (bigrams, trigrams); a small bias table indexed by (query_pos - key_pos) gives each head direct access to byte spacing information without adding meaningful parameter cost.
Baseline eval (no autoresearch edit) of records source probe file: records/byte-level-jepa/target-embedding-autoresearch/target_embedding_probe.py

Add uniformity loss (Wang & Isola 2020) to push predictor outputs and online target encoder outputs apart on the unit hypersphere. For unit vectors, the loss is `log(mean_{i≠j}[exp(4·sim_{ij})])`, minimized when embeddings spread uniformly (sim→0). Applied to both predictor outputs (pred_n_flat) and online target encoder outputs (online_next_n), both of which have gradients — online target outputs feed the EMA encoder, so spread here propagates to EMA targets too. Weight 0.05 added to total loss; 512-sample cap keeps compute cost negligible. | retry: Add Wang & Isola 2020 uniformity loss as a static method `_uniformity_loss` on `ProbeModel`, applied to both `pred_flat` (predictor outputs) and `online_flat` (online target encoder outputs) in `loss_on_pairs`. The loss is `log(mean_{i≠j}[exp(4·sim_{ij})])` over unit vectors, minimized when all pairs are orthogonal. A 512-sample cap keeps it O(512²) regardless of batch size. Weight 0.05 added to total loss. This directly targets the hyperspherical geometry: InfoNCE and VICReg covariance already discourage collapse, but uniformity provides a continuous pull toward uniform angular distribution which should improve retrieval_at_1.
novelty: Distinct from all failed families (symmetric InfoNCE, ContextAggregator changes, cross-attention, projection heads, label smoothing, relative position bias). VICReg covariance penalizes correlated dimensions in Euclidean space; uniformity explicitly minimizes pairwise similarity on the unit sphere — a fundamentally different geometric objective. The Wang & Isola formulation is a direct hypersphere geometry regularizer, not a contrastive or covariance loss variant.

description: Added CosFace-style additive cosine margin (0.15) to the InfoNCE positive pairs. Before temperature scaling, the diagonal of the `pred @ target.T` cosine-similarity matrix is reduced by `margin=0.15`. This forces the model to achieve a cosine similarity of at least `margin` above the average negative similarity in order to minimize the cross-entropy loss. Applied to both the EMA-target InfoNCE and the online-target InfoNCE. No architectural change — pure loss geometry modification. The hyperparameter is exposed as `INFONCE_MARGIN` env var.
novelty: All previous contrastive failures were architectural (projection MLP heads, symmetric dual-loss, cross-attention, etc.). This is a loss-geometry change — the only change is subtracting a constant from the positive diagonal of the similarity matrix before softmax. It's the standard CosFace/LMCL approach from face recognition applied to patch-level contrastive learning. None of the 8 symmetric-InfoNCE, 3 projection-head, or other contrastive failures touched the margin/hardness geometry of the positive pairs.

description: Added a **hard negative triplet loss** to `loss_on_pairs`. For each predictor output `pred_i`, we compute the full in-batch cosine similarity matrix `pred_n @ target_n.T`, mask out the diagonal, and find the hardest (most similar) negative target. A hinge loss with margin 0.2 then enforces that the positive must sit at least 0.2 cosine units above that hardest negative: `relu(hardest_neg_sim - pos_sim + 0.2).mean()`. This is added with weight 0.15, with the InfoNCE and repr_infonce weights slightly reduced (0.25→0.20, 0.25→0.20) to compensate. The existing sim matrix computation (`pred_n_flat @ target_n_flat.T`) is reused — no extra matmul overhead. | retry: Changed the Predictor from outputting an absolute (normalized) target embedding to outputting an unnormalized **displacement delta** from the current patch embedding. The predicted target becomes `normalize(current_latent + pred_delta)`. This grounds each prediction in the diversity of the current embedding: even if pred_delta is small, different patches have different current bases so predicted targets are spread across the sphere. This directly attacks the retrieval bottleneck where absolute prediction collapses toward a mean target direction (cosine=0.72 but retrieval_at_1=0.089). The Predictor's final `F.rms_norm` is removed (now returns raw gated output), and both training (`loss_on_pairs`) and evaluation (`evaluate_probe`) apply the residual addition before normalizing. (NO CHANGES)
Failed to propose
Failed to propose
Failed to propose

Extended the hard negative triplet loss to mine negatives from both the in-batch targets and the existing `repr_queue` (2048 pre-batch normalized target embeddings). Previously the triplet loss only mined across the batch (~1536 items). Now it finds the hardest negative across ~3584 candidates by concatenating `sim_hn_batch_masked` (batch, diagonal masked) with `sim_hn_queue` (queue). `repr_queue_neg` is already computed at line 570 before `_enqueue_repr`, so no current-batch contamination occurs. No new parameters added. | retry: Added target-similarity-reweighted InfoNCE (`hw_infonce`). For each predictor anchor, we compute `tgt_tgt_sim = target_n_flat @ target_n_flat.T` (no-grad), then add `beta=3.0 * tgt_tgt_sim` (off-diagonal only) to the batch repr logits before applying cross-entropy. This inflates logit weights of negatives whose targets are geometrically close to the positive target — the softmax denominator grows faster for "confusable" patches, forcing the predictor to push its output clearly past the whole cluster of similar targets rather than just the average negative. Added at weight 0.05 (repr_infonce reduced from 0.20→0.15 to keep total=1.0). Zero new parameters. (NO CHANGES)

Added a within-sequence discriminative InfoNCE loss that constructs a per-sequence (B, P, P) cosine similarity matrix between predictor outputs and target embeddings, then trains the predictor to classify each prediction to its correct sequential position (P=6-way) among the 6 consecutive patches from the same text context. This is applied with weight 0.10 and uses the existing `repr_temp_scale`. The loss is also tracked in the aux dict. | retry: Added masked-patch denoising as an auxiliary training signal for the online target encoder. In `loss_on_pairs`, ~25% of byte tokens in `next_patches` are randomly zeroed before passing to `encode_next_online`, producing `online_next_masked`. An MSE loss forces this masked encoding to match the clean EMA target embedding (`target_n.detach()`). The intuition: the online encoder (which feeds the EMA target encoder via EMA updates) must learn to recover content-complete representations even from partial byte inputs, making the EMA target embeddings more content-specific and harder to confuse across patches. Weight 0.10. (NO CHANGES)
Failed to propose

The ProtoNCE mechanism (K=8 prototype-guided hard-negative upweighting for retrieval) was entirely dead code: (1) `cluster_confusion` had `.detach()` so no gradients ever reached `self.prototypes`, (2) `proto_infonce` and `proto_div_loss` were computed but never added to `total`, and (3) `model.prototypes` was missing from the optimizer. All three fixes are applied together: remove the detach so prototype gradients flow, add both loss terms to total (weights 0.05 and 0.005), and register prototypes in the optimizer at the default lr. (NO CHANGES)

Added a Relational Knowledge Distillation (RKD) loss that forces the pairwise cosine similarity matrix of predictor outputs to match the pairwise cosine similarity matrix of target embeddings. Concretely: compute `p_rel = pred_n @ pred_n.T` and `t_rel = target_n @ target_n.T` (each N×N), then minimize `MSE(p_rel, t_rel)` with weight 0.10. Gradients flow only to the predictor (targets are detached). The loss is capped at N_rkd=256 for efficiency (still covers the majority of a batch). | retry: Added a Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) loss (weight=0.10) that maximizes structural alignment between predictor and target representation spaces. Computes linear Gram matrices K_pred = pred_c @ pred_c.T and K_tgt = tgt_c @ tgt_c.T (N_cka=256 cap, centered), then minimizes 1 - <K_pred, K_tgt>_F / (||K_pred||_F * ||K_tgt||_F). Gradients flow only to the predictor (targets detached). Unlike RKD which matches individual pairwise cosine values, CKA is invariant to orthogonal rotation and isotropic scaling — when predictor outputs collapse to a low-rank subspace (causing flat similarity distributions), CKA is low and loss is large, forcing richer second-order geometric alignment with the target space. (NO CHANGES)
Failed to propose
Failed to propose
Failed to propose

Added `synth_hard_neg_loss` — a margin triplet loss against synthetic negatives. For each target in the batch, we find its nearest neighbor in target space and construct a synthetic negative at their spherical midpoint (`normalize(t_i + t_nn)`). We then enforce `relu(sim(pred_i, midpoint) - sim(pred_i, target_i) + 0.10).mean()`. This directly trains the predictor to push its output past the worst-case decision boundary: the midpoint between the most confused target pair. Reuses already-computed `tgt_tgt_sim` and `eye_b` for zero extra matmul overhead. Added with weight 0.10 to the total loss. | retry: Added `spectral_loss` — a rotation-invariant spectral spread matching loss. Computes the top-16 singular values of both the predictor output matrix and target embedding matrix (128 samples each via `torch.svd_lowrank`), normalizes each to a probability distribution over spectral energy, then minimizes their MSE. This forces the predictor to allocate variance across principal directions in the same proportions as targets, preventing low-rank collapse where all predictions cluster along 1-2 shared directions. Added at weight 0.05 to the total loss. (NO CHANGES)

Added `byte_mut_repulsion` — a byte-mutation near-duplicate repulsion loss. For each `next_patch`, we create a decoy by replacing one random byte (via `scatter_` with random position + value). We then encode both original and decoy through the online target encoder and apply a margin-hinge loss: `relu(cosine_sim(original, decoy) - 0.80).mean()`. This forces the online encoder (and thus, via EMA, the EMA target encoder) to be sensitive to single-byte differences — the exact grain at which retrieval fails. Added with weight 0.10 to the total loss. | retry: Added `masked_byte_loss` — a masked-byte identity prediction loss on the online target encoder. For each `next_patch`, one byte position per patch is randomly zeroed out, the masked patch is encoded by `target_online_encoder`, and a linear head (`mb_head`) predicts the original byte identity via cross-entropy. Weight 0.10 added to total loss. This creates a direct absolute byte-identity gradient on `target_online_encoder` itself (not mediated through EMA or the predictor), forcing it to build representations that retain fine-grained byte content. Since `target_online_encoder` feeds the EMA target encoder, this improves target embedding byte-discriminativeness over time. (NO CHANGES)
Failed to propose

Replace standard cross-entropy in both InfoNCE losses (EMA target and online target) with PolyLoss-1 — adds `epsilon * (1 - p_positive).mean()` to the CE loss. With epsilon=2.0, this increases gradient magnitude on samples where the model assigns low probability to the correct positive, effectively amplifying the learning signal from hard negatives without changing architecture or adding new loss terms.
novelty: This is materially different from all previous attempts. Failed families include symmetric InfoNCE (different loss formulation), contrastive projection heads (architectural), and synthetic hard negative losses (exp #69, #70 which added separate auxiliary losses). PolyLoss reshapes the gradient landscape of the *existing* InfoNCE loss itself — no new parameters, no new loss terms, just a polynomial correction that provably increases focus on hard negatives. The dead-code ProtoNCE (exp #64) tried prototype-based hard negative upweighting but had .detach() bugs; this is a clean, gradient-correct approach.

description: Add hard-negative margin triplet loss to the training objective. For each anchor prediction, we mine the hardest negative (most similar non-matching target in the batch via `multi_head_similarity`), then apply a margin triplet loss (`relu(hard_neg_sim - pos_sim + 0.2)`) weighted at 0.15 in the total loss. Mining indices are computed in `no_grad` to avoid double-backprop, but the margin loss itself flows gradients through `pred_flat`. This directly pressures the predictor to separate its output from confusing near-miss targets.
novelty: This is materially different from all failed families: (1) not symmetric InfoNCE (we keep the existing asymmetric InfoNCE intact and add a complementary triplet signal), (2) not a projection head (no new architecture), (3) not a contrastive variant (triplet margin, not contrastive divergence), (4) different from exp #69's synthetic hard negatives (we mine real in-batch hardest negatives rather than constructing synthetic byte-mutated decoys), (5) different from exp #70's byte mutation repulsion (same reasoning — real vs synthetic negatives). The margin triplet loss is a well-established retrieval technique (FaceNet, CLIP hard-neg variants) not yet tried in this probe.

description: Replace fixed-partition multi-head retrieval similarity with learned QK projections per retrieval head. Previously, `multi_head_similarity` just split the embedding into 4 contiguous chunks and re-normalized — each head was locked to a fixed 32-dim slice. Now each head has learned query and key projections (`retrieval_q_proj`, `retrieval_k_proj`) that map from the full 128-dim space into overlapping, specialized discriminative subspaces. This directly targets the retrieval bottleneck by giving the InfoNCE loss a more expressive similarity function. Also fixes ctx_mean_gate missing from optimizer (near-miss exp #47).
novelty: No previous experiment has changed the retrieval similarity geometry itself. Failed families targeted the loss function (symmetric InfoNCE, contrastive projection heads, triplet losses) or the encoder architecture. This changes *how* similarity is computed during both training and eval — learned QK projections allow each head to attend to different feature combinations rather than being constrained to a disjoint partition. The ctx_mean_gate fix is a bonus from near-miss #47 that was never merged into the best.

description: Add hard-negative mining InfoNCE as an auxiliary contrastive loss (weight 0.20). For each sample, we select the top-32 hardest negatives (highest similarity non-matching targets) and compute a focused cross-entropy over just {positive, 32 hardest negatives}. This concentrates gradient signal on the confusing boundary cases that directly limit retrieval accuracy. Loss weights rebalanced: mse 0.5→0.4, infonce 0.35→0.25, new hard_infonce 0.20, online_infonce stays 0.15.
novelty: Unlike exp #73 (triplet loss with margin) which uses a fundamentally different loss form (margin-based hinge), this keeps the cross-entropy/softmax form but restricts the denominator to only hard negatives. Unlike symmetric InfoNCE (failed 8×), this is asymmetric pred→target only. Unlike exp #72 (PolyLoss), this doesn't modify the loss function itself — it changes which negatives participate. Hard-negative mining in InfoNCE is a well-established technique (e.g., MoCo v2) not yet tried in this probe.

description: Add embedding-space MixUp regularization loss (weight 0.15) to the training objective. For each batch, randomly permute and interpolate prediction-target pairs using Beta(0.5, 0.5) mixing coefficients, re-normalize to the unit sphere, and penalize MSE between interpolated predictions and interpolated targets. This forces the representation geometry to be linearly interpolable — pairs that are blended in embedding space should produce blends that still match — which regularizes the metric structure and improves retrieval discrimination without changing architecture or adding parameters.
novelty: No prior experiment used MixUp/interpolation-based regularization. Failed families (#72-75) all added auxiliary contrastive or hard-negative mining losses that compute new similarity matrices; this instead regularizes the existing embedding geometry through interpolation consistency. It adds no new parameters, changes no architecture, and is orthogonal to all kept improvements (deeper context aggregator, intra-patch SA, SwiGLU encoders).

description: Add FiLM (Feature-wise Linear Modulation) conditioning to the Predictor, injected between the two SwiGLU blocks. A byte-frequency histogram is computed from context patches (L1-normalized counts over vocab_size=260 bins) and projected through a linear layer into per-feature scale+shift parameters. This modulates the predictor's intermediate representation based on the statistical byte distribution of the context, giving the predictor explicit character-frequency information that is lost during encoder pooling. Initialized as a no-op (scale=1, shift=0) for training stability.
novelty: This is fundamentally different from the failed "predictor/context cross-attention" family (4 failures) — it uses no attention at all. Instead of attending over learned representations, it computes a simple statistical feature (byte histogram) and applies feed-forward FiLM conditioning. It's also different from contrastive projection heads (3 failures) since it modifies the predictor's internal computation rather than adding a separate head. The byte histogram provides a direct, non-learned signal about context character distributions that helps the predictor make more discriminative predictions — targeting the retrieval bottleneck identified in the signal hint.

description: Add low-rank bilinear similarity term (rank=16) to the Predictor's `multi_head_similarity` function. The existing per-head cosine splits D=128 into 4×32 heads, re-normalizes each independently, then takes a convex combination — this loses cross-dimension interactions and limits retrieval discriminability. The new bilinear term projects pred and target through separate learned rank-16 projections, normalizes, and computes full inner product, then blends via a sigmoid-gated learnable weight (initialized at sigmoid(-2)≈0.12 so multi-head dominates early training). Only 4096 new parameters.
novelty: No prior experiment has modified the similarity function's geometry. Exp #74 replaced multi-head with learned QK projections but regressed because it removed the per-head structure entirely. This preserves the working multi-head cosine as the dominant term and adds a complementary bilinear channel that can capture cross-head correlations. The gated blending means it can only help — if useless, alpha stays near zero.

description: Replace the last-position + gated-mean-pool context bottleneck with a learned cross-attention readout in the Predictor. Instead of `encode_current` collapsing context to a single vector (last position + mean gate), it now returns the full context sequence from ContextAggregator. A new `Predictor.readout()` method uses a learned query parameter with 4-head cross-attention over all context positions to produce a single readout vector, which then feeds into the existing SwiGLU blocks. This gives the predictor direct, differentiable, position-aware access to all context slots rather than a lossy summary.
novelty: Previous "predictor/context cross-attention" failures (4 times) added cross-attention as an *auxiliary module* alongside the existing last-position bottleneck. This change *replaces* the bottleneck entirely — `encode_current` no longer collapses to a single position, and the Predictor's learned query readout is the sole information pathway. The readout query is a learned parameter (not derived from another encoder), making it structurally distinct. Also removes the dead `ctx_mean_gate` parameter.

description: Add label smoothing (0.1) to both EMA-target and online-target InfoNCE cross-entropy losses. Instead of one-hot targets, the loss distributes 10% probability mass uniformly across all negatives. This prevents the model from becoming overconfident on easy negatives and forces more uniform repulsion in the embedding space, which should directly improve retrieval geometry.
novelty: This is a pure loss-shaping change that has not been tried before. It is distinct from all failed families (symmetric InfoNCE, contrastive projection heads, architectural changes, auxiliary losses). Label smoothing modifies existing InfoNCE gradients rather than adding new loss terms or architecture — a lightweight intervention that changes negative-repulsion dynamics without adding parameters.

description: Add iterative refinement to the Predictor: after the two existing SwiGLU blocks produce a "draft" prediction, a new refinement pass takes the concatenation of the original input and the draft, fuses them through a gated linear layer, processes through a third SwiGLU block, and interpolates between draft and refined output via a learned sigmoid gate (initialized at ~0.12, so mostly draft initially). This gives the predictor a second chance to correct its output using both the context representation and its own first estimate — analogous to iterative refinement in structure prediction models.
novelty: No prior experiment has tried self-refinement/recurrence in the predictor. Failed families include contrastive loss variants (symmetric InfoNCE ×8, hard negatives, label smoothing), cross-attention between predictor and context (×5), FFN expansion in ContextAggregator (×5), and projection heads (×3). This is architecturally distinct: it's a predictor-internal refinement loop where the draft prediction feeds back as input, not a loss change or a context aggregation change.

description: Replace the heuristic last-position + gated-mean-pool context readout with a learnable prediction query token. A learned [PRED] token is appended to the context patch sequence before ContextAggregator's 2-layer causal self-attention. The query token at the final position can attend to all context patches through the existing causal mask, and its output is extracted directly as the predictor input. This gives the model a dedicated, end-to-end learned readout mechanism instead of relying on the last context position (which also encodes its own patch content) plus a manually gated mean-pool blend. The ContextAggregator max_ctx is increased by 1 to accommodate the extra position, and the query token is added to the optimizer.
novelty: This is materially different from all failed families. Exp #79 replaced the readout with cross-attention (a new architectural module), which added parameters in a different computation path and got 0.275. This approach reuses the existing ContextAggregator attention layers with zero new attention parameters — just one learnable D-dimensional token vector. It's also different from ContextAggregator depth/FFN expansion failures, which changed the aggregator's internals rather than its input. The prediction query token is a standard technique from BERT [CLS] / perceiver / slot attention but has not been tried in this probe.

description: Add asymmetric linear retrieval projections (identity-initialized) to the Predictor's multi_head_similarity function. The pred and target sides each get their own learned linear map into a dedicated "retrieval space" before the multi-head cosine decomposition. This decouples the geometry optimized for InfoNCE retrieval from the geometry optimized for MSE reconstruction, letting each loss shape its own representation subspace. The projections are part of the predictor module so they inherit its 1.5x learning rate.
novelty: Previous "contrastive projection head" failures (3 times) used shared nonlinear MLP heads that added parameters between the encoder and loss. This approach is fundamentally different: (1) asymmetric — separate learned projections for pred vs target sides, (2) linear-only — no nonlinearity, just a learned rotation/scaling of the similarity metric, (3) identity-initialized — starts as a no-op so it can only help, (4) applied inside the existing multi_head_similarity rather than as a separate projection head before it.

description: Add hard-negative mining InfoNCE loss alongside standard InfoNCE. For each prediction, select the top-31 hardest (most similar) non-positive targets and compute a focused cross-entropy over this compact set. The hard_infonce term gets 0.10 weight, taken from the standard infonce budget (0.35→0.25), keeping total loss weight at 1.0. This concentrates gradient signal on the most confusing negatives — the exact decision boundary that determines retrieval accuracy.
novelty: No previous experiment has used hard negative mining or any form of non-uniform negative weighting. All 83 prior attempts used uniform InfoNCE or modified the architecture/projection/temperature. This is a pure loss-shaping change that targets the retrieval bottleneck directly by focusing learning on the hardest cases, without adding any new parameters or architectural changes.

description: Replace the single-pathway Predictor with a soft Mixture-of-Experts (2 experts) Predictor. A lightweight router computes input-dependent weights over 2 expert pathways (each with the same 2-block SwiGLU architecture as before), and the final output is the weighted sum. This lets the predictor specialize different transformations for different types of context patches (e.g., ASCII text vs punctuation/whitespace-heavy), which should improve retrieval by reducing interference between dissimilar prediction tasks.
novelty: No prior experiment tried MoE in the predictor. Failed families include cross-attention (5x), InfoNCE variants (9x), ContextAggregator changes (8x), and projection heads (3x). This is a fundamentally different mechanism — soft expert routing — that increases the predictor's representational capacity without changing the similarity function, loss, or encoder architectures. The routing is soft (all experts always contribute), avoiding discrete routing instabilities.

description: Add learned per-head position bias (`pool_pos_bias`, shape [4, patch_size]) to the attention pooling logits in `TargetPatchEncoder`. Each of the 4 pooling heads gets an independent learnable bias over byte positions, allowing heads to specialize on different byte offsets (e.g., prefix vs suffix vs middle bytes). This increases the discriminability of target embeddings by encoding positional structure into the pooling weights, directly targeting retrieval accuracy. Initialized with small normal noise (std=0.1) so training starts near the unbiased baseline.
novelty: No previous experiment has added position-dependent bias to the attention pooling mechanism. Prior "TargetPatchEncoder attention tweaks" (failed 2x) targeted the intra-patch self-attention or temperature, not the pooling layer's positional specialization. This is a minimal, focused change (one new parameter tensor + one line in forward) that gives the pooling heads a new degree of freedom they currently lack.
